raw_case:
Imaging of Treacher Collins syndrome: A case report
Treacher Collins syndrome, also known as mandibulofacial dysostosis, is a rare congenital disorder affecting craniofacial development. It is caused by an autosomal dominant mutation, primarily in the TCOF1 gene, which impacts the development of the first and second branchial arches. We present the case of a 12-year-old male with bilateral conductive hearing loss and deformed ears, whose clinical and imaging findings were consistent with Treacher Collins syndrome. Imaging revealed microtia, atresia of the external auditory canals, and hypoplastic middle ear structures. Additionally, facial abnormalities such as retrognathia, hypoplastic zygomatic bones, and a cleft palate were identified. This case underscores the crucial role of imaging in diagnosing Treacher Collins syndrome and guiding multidisciplinary management strategies.

Introduction

Treacher Collins syndrome (TCS), also known as mandibulofacial dysostosis, is a rare congenital anomaly with an estimated incidence of about 1 in 50,000 live births [1]. The syndrome was named after Edward Treacher Collins (1862-1932), an English ophthalmologist and surgeon, who described many of its features [1]. TCS is caused by an autosomal dominant genetic mutation, leading to bilateral malformations of the first and second branchial arches [1]. The primary genes involved in its development include TCOF1, which is most commonly affected, along with POLR1C and POLR1D [1,2]. Craniofacial abnormalities in TCS typically involve the zygomatic bones, jaws, palate, and oral cavity, which can result in respiratory and feeding difficulties [3]. Conductive hearing loss is a common presenting symptom due to deformities in the external and middle ear.We present a case of a 12-year-old male with clinical features consistent with TCS. Imaging was advised for further evaluation, and the findings confirmed the diagnosis.

Case presentation

A 12-year-old male presented with bilateral deformed external ears and conductive hearing loss since childhood. All other developmental milestones were normal. The patient had facial disfigurement, including small cheeks. There was no significant family history of similar conditions. His mother did not report any history of teratogenic drug intake during pregnancy. On examination, the patient was found to have bilateral microtia with canal atresia. Clinically, the head was dolichocephalic, with antimongoloid slanting palpebral fissures and sparse eyelashes. Auscultation of the heart revealed normal heart sounds. Audiometric testing showed bilateral severe conductive hearing loss. Despite these challenges, the patient's IQ and speech performances were within the normal range. Based on these clinical findings, a provisional diagnosis of TCS was made, and imaging was recommended for confirmation and further management. The differential diagnosis included Auriculocondylar syndrome, Goldenhar syndrome, and Nager syndrome.A computed tomography (CT) scan of the face and temporal bones (Fig. 1, Fig. 2, Fig. 3, Fig. 4) revealed the absence of both the bony and cartilaginous portions of the external auditory canal, along with deformed pinnae. Bilateral middle ear cavities appeared hypoplastic with absent ossicles, and bilateral mastoids were hypoplastic and poorly pneumatized. The inner ears were grossly normal. Additional findings included a downward slant of the superior orbital walls, hypoplastic facial bones, and hypoplastic bilateral zygoma with poorly formed zygomatic arches. There was crowding of the teeth in both jaws. The condylar and coronoid processes of the mandible were normal. A small cleft palate was noted on the posterior hard palate, with normal bilateral choanae. There was a retrognathia, a hypoplastic mandible (micrognathia), and an obtuse mandibular angle. Left-sided deviation of the nasal septum with a bony spur was also observed. The visible portions of the paranasal sinuses were normal. Based on these clinical and imaging findings, a diagnosis of TCS was made.The patient's parents declined genetic testing due to financial limitations. The patient was scheduled for the placement of a bone-anchored hearing aid to correct the conductive hearing loss.

Discussion

In TCS, imaging studies typically reveal distinct craniofacial abnormalities that are often bilateral and symmetric. Common findings in the dental and mandibular regions include retrognathia and micrognathia, where the jaw is underdeveloped, leading to a receding chin. Hypoplasia or aplasia of the condylar and coronoid processes of the mandible may also be present. Additionally, there may be marked bowing of the lower border of the mandible and a concave curvature of the horizontal ramus, which is considered pathognomonic for the syndrome. The maxilla may be narrow, often accompanied by an elevated or narrow palate. Associated findings may include cleft palate, dental malocclusion, and macrostomia. The zygomatic arch is frequently malformed, underdeveloped, or absent, contributing to facial asymmetry [1,3,4].The findings in the ear include microtia (small or malformed external ears) and aplasia of the external auditory meatus. The middle ear structures may also be involved, with hypoplasia or aplasia of the ossicles and the middle ear cavity, contributing to conductive hearing loss [1,3,4].Ocular findings in TCS include downward slanting palpebral fissures, often due to a lack of support from the underdeveloped zygoma. The lower inner third of the eyelids may also be absent. Other ocular anomalies can include absent eyelashes, notched irises, notched choroid, and colobomas. Nasal abnormalities, such as obliteration of the nasofrontal angle leading to narrow nares, are also commonly seen. Hypoplasia of the alar cartilages and paranasal sinuses, along with choanal atresia, may be present in some cases [1,3,4]. Reduced upper airway volume and severe narrowing of the retroglossal space can result in apnea, along with speech and hearing difficulties [5].Differential diagnoses for TCS include Auriculocondylar syndrome, Goldenhar syndrome, and Nager syndrome. Auriculocondylar syndrome presents with prominent malformed auricles, often described as "question mark ear," and specific mandibular condyle hypoplasia or aplasia [1]. In contrast, Goldenhar syndrome typically has ear anomalies, such as preauricular tags and otic hypoplasia, along with ocular anomalies like unilateral microphthalmia or epibulbar dermoids, and skeletal issues like hemifacial microsomia and vertebral anomalies [6]. Nager syndrome involves similar facial features to TCS, including mandibular hypoplasia and micrognathia, but it also includes skeletal anomalies, such as radial hypoplasia, thumb malformations, and syndactyly, which are not present in TCS [7].Management of TCS requires a multidisciplinary approach, including reconstructive surgery to address facial abnormalities, bone conduction hearing aids or surgical correction for conductive hearing loss, and speech therapy for language development [4,8].

Conclusion

Clinical evaluation, along with correlated imaging findings, is crucial for the diagnosis of TCS. Moreover, imaging provides essential insights for surgical planning and the management of associated impairments, aiding in a comprehensive treatment approach.

Patient consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient's parents for publication of this case report and any accompanying images.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 12-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint:** Bilateral deformed external ears and bilateral conductive hearing loss.
- **History of Present Illness:** Conductive hearing loss since childhood. All other developmental milestones were normal.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** No reported maternal teratogenic exposure during pregnancy.
- **Family History:** None significant for similar conditions.
- **Physical Examination:** 
  - Bilateral microtia with canal atresia.
  - Dolichocephalic head.
  - Antimongoloid slanting palpebral fissures.
  - Sparse eyelashes.
  - Facial disfigurement, including small cheeks.
  - Normal heart sounds.
  - IQ and speech within normal range.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Audiometry: Bilateral severe conductive hearing loss.
  - Imaging (CT): 
    - Microtia and external auditory canal atresia bilaterally.
    - Hypoplastic middle ear cavities with absent ossicles.
    - Dolichocephalic skull, downward slant of superior orbital walls, hypoplastic zygomatic bones, and poorly formed zygomatic arches.
    - Retrognathia and hypoplastic mandible (micrognathia).
    - Small cleft in the posterior hard palate.
    - Normal inner ear structures, visible paranasal sinuses, and mandibular condyles/coronoid processes.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Treacher Collins Syndrome (TCS):** 
   - Considered first due to clinical features of bilateral ear deformities, facial asymmetry (micrognathia, hypoplastic zygomatic bones), cleft palate, and conductive hearing loss. Imaging findings confirm typical craniofacial abnormalities consistent with TCS.
2. **Auriculocondylar Syndrome:** 
   - Excluded due to the absence of hallmark features like "question mark ear" and mandibular condyle anomalies.
3. **Goldenhar Syndrome:** 
   - Excluded because the patient lacks unilateral facial asymmetry, preauricular tags, ocular dermoids, and vertebral anomalies.
4. **Nager Syndrome:**
   - Excluded as there are no notable limb anomalies such as radial hypoplasia or syndactyly, which are characteristic of Nager syndrome.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Treacher Collins Syndrome (TCS) based on typical clinical presentation and imaging findings.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the key features of this case. The patient is a 12-year-old male with bilateral microtia and external auditory canal atresia, conductive hearing loss since childhood. Other physical findings include dolichocephalic head, antimongoloid slanting eyes, sparse eyelashes, facial dysmorphism like small cheeks, retrognathia, and a small cleft in the posterior hard palate. Imaging shows hypoplastic middle ear structures, absent ossicles, and issues with zygomatic bones and mandible.

First, the bilateral microtia and canal atresia with conductive hearing loss point towards a congenital syndrome. Since the inner ear is normal, the hearing loss is conductive, which aligns with the middle ear abnormalities (absent ossicles, hypoplastic cavities). This makes me think of syndromes that affect the first and second branchial arches, as those structures develop from there.

Next, the facial features are significant. Antimongoloid slant (downward slanting palpebral fissures), dolichocephaly, zygomatic hypoplasia, micrognathia. These craniofacial anomalies are classic for Treacher Collins syndrome (TCS), also known as mandibulofacial dysostosis. TCS is characterized by underdeveloped facial bones, especially the zygomas and mandible, downslanting eyes, colobomas (not mentioned here though), and ear abnormalities. The presence of a small cleft in the posterior hard palate might be a submucous cleft, which can occur in TCS.

Another possibility could be Goldenhar syndrome (hemifacial microsomia), but that's usually unilateral and includes epibulbar dermoids, which aren't noted here. The bilateral involvement here makes TCS more likely. Also, the family history is non-significant, but TCS can be autosomal dominant, though many cases are de novo mutations. The normal IQ and speech are consistent with TCS, as intelligence is typically unaffected.

The imaging findings like hypoplastic zygomatic bones, absent ossicles, and mandibular hypoplasia all fit with Treacher Collins. The absence of heart defects (normal heart sounds) and other organ involvement also supports TCS, which primarily affects craniofacial structures. The small posterior cleft palate could be part of the spectrum. Other differentials like Nager syndrome have limb abnormalities, which aren't mentioned here. So, putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is Treacher Collins syndrome.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with bilateral microtia, external auditory canal atresia, and severe conductive hearing loss. These findings suggest a congenital disorder affecting the development of the first and second branchial arches, which form the external/middle ear and associated craniofacial structures. The normal inner ear on imaging explains the conductive (rather than sensorineural) hearing loss.

<step 2> The craniofacial anomalies—dolichocephalic skull, antimongoloid palpebral fissures, hypoplastic zygomatic bones, retrognathia, and micrognathia—indicate a syndromic diagnosis. These features align with mandibulofacial dysostosis, specifically Treacher Collins syndrome (TCS), which is characterized by bilateral zygomatic, mandibular, and ear malformations. The downward-slanting orbital walls and sparse eyelashes further support this.

<step 3> The posterior hard palate cleft (submucous or incomplete) and the absence of systemic abnormalities (normal heart, IQ, and speech) help exclude other syndromes. Goldenhar syndrome (oculo-auriculo-vertebral spectrum) typically involves unilateral ear anomalies and vertebral defects, while Nager syndrome includes limb abnormalities, neither of which are present here. The lack of maternal teratogen exposure and normal developmental milestones also favor a genetic etiology like TCS.

<step 4> The CT findings of hypoplastic middle ear cavities, absent ossicles, and mandibular/zygomatic underdevelopment are pathognomonic for Treacher Collins syndrome. The autosomal dominant inheritance pattern (often de novo mutations in *TCOF1*, *POLR1D*, or *POLR1C* genes) explains the sporadic presentation despite a negative family history.

### Answer:
Treacher Collins syndrome (mandibulofacial dysostosis)

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with bilateral microtia, external auditory canal atresia, and severe conductive hearing loss. These findings suggest a congenital disorder affecting the development of the first and second branchial arches, which form the external/middle ear and associated craniofacial structures. The normal inner ear on imaging explains the conductive (rather than sensorineural) hearing loss.

The craniofacial anomalies—dolichocephalic skull, antimongoloid palpebral fissures, hypoplastic zygomatic bones, retrognathia, and micrognathia—indicate a syndromic diagnosis. These features align with mandibulofacial dysostosis, specifically Treacher Collins syndrome (TCS), which is characterized by bilateral zygomatic, mandibular, and ear malformations. The downward-slanting orbital walls and sparse eyelashes further support this.

The posterior hard palate cleft (submucous or incomplete) and the absence of systemic abnormalities (normal heart, IQ, and speech) help exclude other syndromes. Goldenhar syndrome (oculo-auriculo-vertebral spectrum) typically involves unilateral ear anomalies and vertebral defects, while Nager syndrome includes limb abnormalities, neither of which are present here. The lack of maternal teratogen exposure and normal developmental milestones also favor a genetic etiology like TCS.

The CT findings of hypoplastic middle ear cavities, absent ossicles, and mandibular/zygomatic underdevelopment are pathognomonic for Treacher Collins syndrome. The autosomal dominant inheritance pattern (often de novo mutations in *TCOF1*, *POLR1D*, or *POLR1C* genes) explains the sporadic presentation despite a negative family history.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Consider Treacher Collins Syndrome (TCS) due to bilateral ear deformities, facial asymmetry (micrognathia, hypoplastic zygomatic bones), cleft palate, and conductive hearing loss; imaging confirms typical craniofacial abnormalities consistent with TCS.
Step 2: Exclude Auriculocondylar Syndrome due to the absence of hallmark features like "question mark ear" and mandibular condyle anomalies.
Step 3: Exclude Goldenhar Syndrome because the patient lacks unilateral facial asymmetry, preauricular tags, ocular dermoids, and vertebral anomalies.
Step 4: Exclude Nager Syndrome as there are no notable limb anomalies such as radial hypoplasia or syndactyly, which are characteristic of Nager syndrome.
Step 5: Diagnose Treacher Collins Syndrome (TCS) based on typical clinical presentation and imaging findings.
